nodes.en: The natural science of living organisms and life processes, spanning molecular mechanisms, cells, heredity, evolution, and the interactions of organisms with each other and their environment. Sibling to Physics and Chemistry (the non-living physical world) and Earth Sciences and Astronomy; it groups the sub-disciplines from Molecular Biology through Ecology and Neurobiology.
nodes.cs: Přírodní věda o živých organismech a životních procesech, zahrnující molekulární mechanismy, buňky, dědičnost, evoluci a vztahy organismů mezi sebou i s prostředím. Sourozenec fyziky a chemie (neživý fyzikální svět), věd o Zemi a astronomie; sdružuje obory od molekulární biologie po ekologii a neurobiologii.
nodes.brief:
Biology is the natural science of living organisms and life processes: the molecular mechanisms that run inside a cell, the heredity that carries information between generations, the evolution that shapes populations over time, and the interactions between organisms and their environment. It spans scales from a single molecule to an entire ecosystem, unified by the fact that its subject is, in every case, something alive.

Biology sits beside [[01.02]] and [[01.01]] within the Natural Sciences — chemistry supplies the reactions of metabolism and genetics, physics the energy and force budget that biophysics applies to living systems — while biology itself asks how those processes combine into organisms that grow, reproduce and evolve. Its subfields divide by scale and question: [[01.03.01]] studies the molecules — DNA, RNA, proteins — that carry out cellular processes, [[01.03.04]] asks how populations change over generations under selection and drift, and [[01.03.05]] studies how organisms interact with each other and their environment at the level of populations, communities and ecosystems.

Evolutionary theory is the thread that ties biology's other subfields together: [[01.03.04]] explains why molecular mechanisms ([[01.03.01]]) and ecological relationships ([[01.03.05]]) take the particular forms observed today. For further reading, see the [Encyclopaedia Britannica entry on biology](https://www.britannica.com/science/biology) and the [NCBI Bookshelf](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/) of open biology and biomedical texts.

nodes.en: The study of biological activity at the level of macromolecules — the structure, function, and interactions of DNA, RNA, and proteins, and the flow of genetic information through replication, transcription, and translation. Narrower than Cell Biology (whole-cell organization) and distinct from Genetics (inheritance patterns): its unit is the molecule and its mechanism.
nodes.cs: Studium biologické činnosti na úrovni makromolekul — struktury, funkce a interakcí DNA, RNA a proteinů a toku genetické informace replikací, transkripcí a translací. Užší než buněčná biologie (organizace celé buňky) a odlišné od genetiky (vzorce dědičnosti): jeho jednotkou je molekula a její mechanismus.
nodes.brief:
Molecular biology studies life at the level of its macromolecules — the structure, function, and interaction of DNA, RNA, and proteins, and the mechanisms by which genetic information is replicated, transcribed, and translated into the machinery of the cell. It sits at the mechanistic core of [[01.03]] Biology: where [[01.03.03]] Genetics tracks how traits are inherited across generations, molecular biology explains the chemistry that makes inheritance possible in the first place — the double helix, the ribosome, the enzymes that copy and repair DNA.

The field's boundary with [[01.03.02]] Cell Biology is one of scale rather than subject: cell biology treats the cell as an organized whole — organelles, membranes, division — while molecular biology zooms into the molecules that whole is built from and the reactions that drive it. Its findings underpin [[01.03.08]] Microbiology's account of bacterial and viral gene expression, and evolutionary claims about how mutations arise and propagate trace back to molecular mechanisms described here, connecting to [[01.03.04]] Evolutionary Biology.

Central techniques — PCR, sequencing, recombinant DNA, CRISPR gene editing — originated in molecular biology and now permeate the rest of the life sciences and biotechnology. For a canonical textbook treatment, see the NCBI Bookshelf edition of [Molecular Biology of the Cell](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK21054/), and for current primary research, the [Nature journal Molecular Cell Biology reviews](https://www.nature.com/subjects/molecular-biology).

nodes.en: The study of the cell as the fundamental unit of life — its organelles, membranes, internal architecture, division, signaling, and metabolism. Broader in scope than Molecular Biology (individual macromolecules) yet narrower than the organism-level view; where Genetics tracks heredity, Cell Biology examines the living machinery that houses and executes it.
nodes.cs: Studium buňky jako základní jednotky života — jejích organel, membrán, vnitřní architektury, dělení, signalizace a metabolismu. Širší záběr než molekulární biologie (jednotlivé makromolekuly), avšak užší než pohled na úrovni organismu; kde genetika sleduje dědičnost, buněčná biologie zkoumá živou mašinerii, jež ji uchovává a vykonává.
nodes.brief:
Cell biology studies the cell as the fundamental unit of life — its organelles, membranes, internal architecture, division cycle, signaling pathways, and metabolism. As a branch of [[01.03]] Biology it occupies the level between molecule and organism: broader than [[01.03.01]] Molecular Biology's focus on individual macromolecules, narrower than the whole-organism perspective of [[01.03.06]] Zoology or [[01.03.07]] Botany.

Cell biology examines the living machinery — the nucleus, mitochondria, cytoskeleton — that houses, replicates, and executes hereditary information moment to moment, complementing the transmission-level account given by genetics. Its treatment of cell division and signaling also underlies [[01.03.10]] Neurobiology's description of how specialized nerve cells communicate, since neurons are, first, cells subject to the same organelle-level rules.

Modern cell biology relies heavily on microscopy and fluorescent labeling to observe processes inside living cells in real time, a methodological line traceable to the original cell theory of the nineteenth century. For a standard reference text, see the NCBI Bookshelf edition of [Molecular Biology of the Cell](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK21054/), and for a public overview of cell structure and function, the [American Society for Cell Biology](https://www.ascb.org/).

nodes.en: The science of heredity and variation — how traits are encoded in genes, transmitted across generations, expressed, and altered by mutation and recombination. Distinct from Molecular Biology (molecular mechanism) and Evolutionary Biology (change over deep time): its focus is inheritance and the transmission of genetic information between generations.
nodes.cs: Věda o dědičnosti a proměnlivosti — jak jsou znaky kódovány v genech, přenášeny mezi generacemi, projevovány a měněny mutací a rekombinací. Odlišná od molekulární biologie (molekulární mechanismus) a evoluční biologie (změny v hlubokém čase): jejím těžištěm je dědičnost a přenos genetické informace mezi generacemi.
nodes.brief:
Genetics is the science of heredity and variation — how traits are encoded in genes, transmitted across generations, expressed in an organism, and altered by mutation and recombination. Within [[01.03]] Biology it is distinguished from [[01.03.01]] Molecular Biology, which explains the chemical mechanism of gene expression, by its focus on the pattern of transmission itself: which traits pass from parent to offspring, and with what probability.

Genetics also differs from [[01.03.04]] Evolutionary Biology in timescale and unit: genetics explains inheritance within a lineage from one generation to the next, while evolutionary biology explains how the frequency of those inherited variants shifts across populations over deep time — the two fields meet in population genetics, where Mendelian inheritance and natural selection are analyzed together. Its clinical and agricultural applications connect it to biotechnology built on tools originating in molecular biology, such as sequencing and gene editing.

Modern genetics spans classical Mendelian inheritance, cytogenetics, and genomics — the study of entire genomes rather than single genes. For an authoritative primer, see the [National Human Genome Research Institute's Genetics glossary and resources](https://www.genome.gov/genetics-glossary), and for genomic data and tools, the [NCBI Genetics resources](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/guide/genetics-genomics/).

nodes.en: The study of how populations and species change over generations through natural selection, genetic drift, mutation, and speciation, and of the shared ancestry linking all life. Where Genetics explains inheritance within lineages, Evolutionary Biology explains descent, adaptation, and diversification across deep time and among taxa.
nodes.cs: Studium toho, jak se populace a druhy mění během generací přirozeným výběrem, genetickým driftem, mutacemi a speciací, a společného původu spojujícího veškerý život. Kde genetika vysvětluje dědičnost uvnitř linií, evoluční biologie vysvětluje původ, adaptaci a diverzifikaci v hlubokém čase a mezi taxony.
nodes.brief:
Evolutionary biology studies how populations and species change over generations through natural selection, genetic drift, mutation, and speciation, and the shared ancestry that links all life. It is the synthesizing subfield of [[01.03]] Biology: where [[01.03.03]] Genetics explains inheritance within a lineage, evolutionary biology explains descent, adaptation, and diversification across lineages and over deep time, drawing its raw material — mutation and recombination — directly from genetics.

Its explanatory reach extends across the other biology subfields: the taxonomic organization used in [[01.03.06]] Zoology and botany reflects evolutionary relationships, and [[01.03.05]] Ecology's account of species interactions is inseparable from the evolutionary pressures — competition, predation, mutualism — that shaped those interactions. Phylogenetics, the reconstruction of evolutionary trees from morphological and molecular data, is one of the field's principal tools and one of its main points of contact with [[01.03.01]] Molecular Biology.

Evidence for evolutionary theory ranges from the fossil record to comparative genomics and direct observation of selection in real time. For an accessible primer built for educators and the public, see UC Berkeley's [Understanding Evolution](https://evolution.berkeley.edu/), and for the primary scientific record, the journal [Evolution](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/journal/15585646).

nodes.en: The study of the relationships between organisms and their environment — populations, communities, ecosystems, energy flow, and nutrient cycles. Unlike the other biology siblings that dissect the organism internally, Ecology looks outward at distribution, abundance, and interaction across scales from individual to biosphere.
nodes.cs: Studium vztahů mezi organismy a jejich prostředím — populací, společenstev, ekosystémů, toku energie a koloběhu živin. Na rozdíl od ostatních biologických oborů, které pitvají organismus zevnitř, ekologie hledí ven na rozšíření, početnost a interakce v měřítcích od jedince po biosféru.
nodes.brief:
Ecology studies the relationships between organisms and their environment — populations, communities, ecosystems, energy flow, and nutrient cycles. Within [[01.03]] Biology it is the outward-looking subfield: where [[01.03.02]] Cell Biology and [[01.03.01]] Molecular Biology dissect the organism internally, ecology examines distribution, abundance, and interaction across scales from the individual to the biosphere.

Ecological patterns are, at root, evolutionary outcomes: predator-prey dynamics, competition, and symbiosis studied in ecology are the selective pressures that [[01.03.04]] Evolutionary Biology treats as drivers of adaptation, so the two fields share much of their conceptual vocabulary. [[01.03.09]] Marine Biology applies this same ecological framework specifically to ocean ecosystems, while the population and community dynamics ecology studies play out concretely in the animal and plant populations of zoology and botany.

Contemporary ecology increasingly addresses applied questions — climate change, biodiversity loss, conservation planning — using tools from population modeling to remote sensing. For a scientific society's overview of the field, see the [Ecological Society of America](https://www.esa.org/), and for open ecological data and modeling resources, the [NEON (National Ecological Observatory Network)](https://www.neonscience.org/).

nodes.en: The branch of biology devoted to animals — their anatomy, physiology, behavior, classification, distribution, and evolution, across the full range from invertebrates to vertebrates. Parallel to Botany (plants) and Microbiology (microbes); it is the taxon-defined study of the animal kingdom rather than a process or level of organization.
nodes.cs: Obor biologie věnovaný živočichům — jejich anatomii, fyziologii, chování, klasifikaci, rozšíření a evoluci, v celé škále od bezobratlých po obratlovce. Souběžný s botanikou (rostliny) a mikrobiologií (mikroorganismy); jde o taxonem vymezené studium živočišné říše, nikoli o proces či úroveň organizace.
nodes.brief:
Zoology is the branch of biology devoted to animals — their anatomy, physiology, behavior, classification, distribution, and evolution, across the full range from invertebrates to vertebrates. Within [[01.03]] Biology it is defined by taxon rather than by process or level of organization, standing parallel to [[01.03.07]] Botany, which covers plants, and microbiology, which covers microbes.

The classification and phylogeny at the heart of zoology are built on the framework supplied by [[01.03.04]] Evolutionary Biology, which explains why animal lineages are related as they are, while the study of animal behavior and physiology draws on [[01.03.05]] Ecology to explain how species interact with their habitats and each other. [[01.03.09]] Marine Biology overlaps with zoology wherever the animals in question are marine, differing in that it is bounded by habitat rather than by kingdom.

Zoology ranges from comparative anatomy and animal behavior (ethology) to conservation biology aimed at protecting endangered species. For a comprehensive species-level reference, see the [Encyclopedia of Life](https://eol.org/), and for the professional scientific record, the [Journal of Zoology](https://zslpublications.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/journal/14697998).

nodes.en: The branch of biology devoted to plants — their structure, physiology, reproduction, classification, ecology, and evolution, including algae and often fungi historically. Parallel to Zoology (animals) and Microbiology (microbes); it is the taxon-defined study of the plant kingdom and photosynthetic life.
nodes.cs: Obor biologie věnovaný rostlinám — jejich stavbě, fyziologii, rozmnožování, klasifikaci, ekologii a evoluci, včetně řas a historicky často i hub. Souběžný se zoologií (živočichové) a mikrobiologií (mikroorganismy); jde o taxonem vymezené studium rostlinné říše a fotosyntetického života.
nodes.brief:
Botany is the branch of biology devoted to plants — their structure, physiology, reproduction, classification, ecology, and evolution, including algae and, historically, fungi. Within [[01.03]] Biology it is defined by taxon, standing parallel to [[01.03.06]] Zoology, which covers animals, and microbiology, which covers microscopic life — together these taxon-based subfields divide the living world by kingdom rather than by process.

Plant classification rests on the phylogenetic relationships established by [[01.03.04]] Evolutionary Biology, and the study of plant communities and their interaction with soil, climate, and other organisms draws directly on [[01.03.05]] Ecology. Photosynthesis and plant metabolism connect botany to the molecular and cellular mechanisms studied elsewhere in biology, since chloroplasts and cell walls are botany's most distinctive cellular structures.

Botany underpins agriculture, forestry, and pharmacognosy, and its herbaria remain a primary evidence base for plant taxonomy and conservation. For an authoritative institutional resource, see the [Royal Botanic Gardens, Kew](https://www.kew.org/science), and for global plant name and distribution data, the [World Flora Online](http://www.worldfloraonline.org/).

nodes.en: The study of microscopic organisms — bacteria, archaea, viruses, fungi, and protists — their physiology, genetics, ecology, and roles in disease, industry, and biogeochemical cycles. Distinguished from Zoology and Botany by scale and taxonomic breadth: it covers all life too small to see with the unaided eye.
nodes.cs: Studium mikroskopických organismů — bakterií, archeí, virů, hub a prvoků — jejich fyziologie, genetiky, ekologie a role v nemocech, průmyslu a biogeochemických cyklech. Od zoologie a botaniky se liší měřítkem a taxonomickou šíří: pokrývá veškerý život příliš malý na pozorování pouhým okem.
nodes.brief:
Microbiology studies microscopic organisms — bacteria, archaea, viruses, fungi, and protists — their physiology, genetics, ecology, and roles in disease, industry, and biogeochemical cycles. Within [[01.03]] Biology it is distinguished from zoology and [[01.03.07]] Botany by scale and taxonomic breadth rather than by a single kingdom: it covers all life too small to see with the unaided eye, cutting across multiple domains of life.

Much of molecular biology's founding experimental work was done in microbial model systems, so microbiology and [[01.03.01]] Molecular Biology share deep methodological roots — bacterial genetics remains a standard route into gene expression and regulation. Microbial ecology and the study of biogeochemical cycles connect the field to [[01.03.05]] Ecology, while pathogen evolution and antibiotic resistance are explained through the lens of [[01.03.04]] Evolutionary Biology.

Microbiology underlies clinical medicine, food safety, and biotechnology, from fermentation to antibiotic production and genetic engineering vectors. For a professional scientific overview, see the [American Society for Microbiology](https://asm.org/), and for a standard reference text, the NCBI Bookshelf edition of [Medical Microbiology](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK7627/).

nodes.en: The study of organisms that live in the sea and of marine ecosystems — from plankton and coral reefs to deep-sea communities — and the physical and chemical factors that shape ocean life. An environment-defined subfield rather than a taxon: it draws on zoology, botany, and ecology but is bounded by the marine habitat, distinct from freshwater Hydrology's physical focus.
nodes.cs: Studium organismů žijících v moři a mořských ekosystémů — od planktonu a korálových útesů po hlubinná společenstva — a fyzikálních a chemických faktorů, jež formují mořský život. Podobor vymezený prostředím, nikoli taxonem: čerpá ze zoologie, botaniky i ekologie, ale je ohraničen mořským biotopem.
nodes.brief:
Marine biology studies organisms that live in the sea and the marine ecosystems they form — from plankton and coral reefs to deep-sea communities — together with the physical and chemical factors that shape ocean life. Within [[01.03]] Biology it is an environment-defined subfield rather than a taxon-defined one: it draws on [[01.03.06]] Zoology, botany, and microbiology for the organisms it studies, but is bounded by the marine habitat rather than by kingdom.

Marine biology applies the community- and ecosystem-level framework of [[01.03.05]] Ecology specifically to ocean environments — nutrient cycling, food webs, and species distribution in a medium governed by currents, salinity, and light penetration rather than terrestrial geography. Evolutionary questions about how life first arose and diversified in the ocean, and how marine and terrestrial lineages diverged, connect it to [[01.03.04]] Evolutionary Biology.

Marine biology is central to fisheries science, climate research (ocean carbon cycling, coral bleaching), and conservation of threatened marine species. For authoritative oceanographic and marine-life data, see the U.S. [National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA)](https://www.noaa.gov/education/resource-collections/marine-life), and for a global species inventory, the [World Register of Marine Species (WoRMS)](https://www.marinespecies.org/).

nodes.en: The study of the nervous system — neurons, synapses, neural circuits, and the biological basis of sensation, behavior, and cognition. Where Cell Biology treats cells generically, Neurobiology focuses on the specialized cells and networks that transmit and process information, from molecular signaling to whole-brain function.
nodes.cs: Studium nervové soustavy — neuronů, synapsí, nervových okruhů a biologického základu vjemů, chování a poznávání. Kde buněčná biologie pojímá buňky obecně, neurobiologie se zaměřuje na specializované buňky a sítě, jež přenášejí a zpracovávají informace, od molekulární signalizace po funkci celého mozku.
nodes.brief:
Neurobiology studies the nervous system — neurons, synapses, neural circuits, and the biological basis of sensation, behavior, and cognition. Within [[01.03]] Biology it specializes [[01.03.02]] Cell Biology's generic account of cellular structure and signaling to a single, highly differentiated cell type: where cell biology treats cells generically, neurobiology focuses on the specialized cells and networks that transmit and process information, from molecular signaling to whole-brain function.

Molecular events — ion channel gating, neurotransmitter synthesis and release — link neurobiology closely to [[01.03.01]] Molecular Biology, while questions about how nervous systems evolved across animal lineages, from simple nerve nets to centralized brains, connect it to [[01.03.04]] Evolutionary Biology and to the comparative anatomy tradition of [[01.03.06]] Zoology.

Neurobiology spans molecular and cellular neuroscience through systems neuroscience to cognitive and behavioral studies, and underlies clinical neurology and psychiatry. For a standard reference text, see the NCBI Bookshelf edition of [Neuroscience](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK10799/), and for the professional scientific society, the [Society for Neuroscience](https://www.sfn.org/).

nodes.name: Biological Disciplines
nodes.en: The life sciences organized by discipline — from molecular, cell and developmental biology through genetics, genomics, evolution and ecology to physiology, neuroscience, immunology, microbiology and the systems, structural and astrobiological frontiers. A deep authoritative map of biology's branches, their core theory, and their active research fronts.

nodes.name: Molecular Biology
nodes.en: Molecular biology studies the molecular mechanisms of gene expression and inheritance — DNA replication and repair, transcription, translation, and the regulatory and epigenetic controls that govern when and how genes are expressed — along with the techniques used to manipulate nucleic acids and genomes. It provides the mechanistic core that Genetics and Genomics build on for inheritance patterns and genome-scale analysis, and that Cell Biology extends to whole-cell organization.
nodes.cs: Molekulární biologie studuje molekulární mechanismy genové exprese a dědičnosti - replikaci a opravu DNA, transkripci, translaci a regulační i epigenetické mechanismy řídící, kdy a jak jsou geny exprimovány - spolu s technikami manipulace s nukleovými kyselinami a genomy. Poskytuje mechanistické jádro, na němž genetika a genomika stavějí své studium dědičnosti a analýzy na úrovni celého genomu a které buněčná biologie rozšiřuje na organizaci celé buňky.

nodes.name: DNA Replication, Repair, and Recombination
nodes.en: Covers the enzymatic machinery that duplicates the genome with high fidelity each cell cycle and the pathways that detect and correct DNA damage. Includes origin licensing, the replisome, mismatch repair, excision repair, and homologous and non-homologous recombination. Fidelity and repair fidelity underlie genome stability, aging, and cancer etiology.
nodes.brief:
Covers the enzymatic machinery that duplicates the genome with high fidelity each cell cycle and the pathways that detect and correct DNA damage. Includes origin licensing, the replisome, mismatch repair, excision repair, and homologous and non-homologous recombination. Fidelity and repair fidelity underlie genome stability, aging, and cancer etiology.

### Concepts
- **Replisome Architecture** — The multiprotein complex of helicase, primase, and DNA polymerases that unwinds and copies both strands at the replication fork.
- **Origin Licensing** — Assembly of pre-replication complexes at defined origins ensures each genomic locus replicates exactly once per cell cycle.
- **Mismatch Repair** — A post-replicative surveillance system excises misincorporated bases missed by polymerase proofreading, cutting error rates roughly a thousandfold.
- **Nucleotide and Base Excision Repair** — Distinct pathways remove bulky adducts or single damaged bases, respectively, restoring the correct sequence using the intact complementary strand as template.
- **Homologous Recombination** — Uses a sister chromatid or homologous sequence as template to accurately repair double-strand breaks and to generate genetic diversity via crossing over.
- **Telomere Maintenance** — Telomerase extends chromosome ends to offset the end-replication problem, linking telomere length to cellular senescence and immortalization.

nodes.name: Transcription and RNA Synthesis
nodes.en: Studies how RNA polymerases convert DNA templates into RNA transcripts, including promoter recognition, elongation, termination, and co-transcriptional RNA processing. Covers the distinct machineries of bacterial and eukaryotic systems and the general and sequence-specific factors that position polymerase at the correct start site. This is the first and most heavily regulated step of gene expression.
nodes.brief:
Studies how RNA polymerases convert DNA templates into RNA transcripts, including promoter recognition, elongation, termination, and co-transcriptional RNA processing. Covers the distinct machineries of bacterial and eukaryotic systems and the general and sequence-specific factors that position polymerase at the correct start site. This is the first and most heavily regulated step of gene expression.

### Concepts
- **RNA Polymerase Structure and Catalysis** — Multi-subunit enzymes catalyze template-directed RNA synthesis using a two-metal-ion mechanism conserved across all domains of life.
- **Promoter Recognition and Initiation** — General transcription factors assemble a pre-initiation complex at core promoter elements to position polymerase and open the DNA duplex.
- **Transcriptional Elongation and Termination** — Elongation factors modulate polymerase pausing and processivity, while intrinsic or factor-dependent mechanisms release the completed transcript.
- **Pre-mRNA Splicing** — The spliceosome removes introns and ligates exons co-transcriptionally, and alternative splicing generates multiple protein isoforms from one gene.
- **5' Capping and 3' Polyadenylation** — Co-transcriptional addition of a 5' cap and 3' poly(A) tail protects the transcript and licenses it for nuclear export and translation.

nodes.name: Translation and Protein Synthesis
nodes.en: Examines how the ribosome decodes messenger RNA into polypeptide chains using the genetic code, transfer RNAs, and a suite of initiation, elongation, and release factors. Addresses translational fidelity, ribosome structure, and co-translational quality-control mechanisms that couple protein synthesis to folding and localization. Central to understanding how genetic information becomes functional protein.
nodes.brief:
Examines how the ribosome decodes messenger RNA into polypeptide chains using the genetic code, transfer RNAs, and a suite of initiation, elongation, and release factors. Addresses translational fidelity, ribosome structure, and co-translational quality-control mechanisms that couple protein synthesis to folding and localization. Central to understanding how genetic information becomes functional protein.

### Concepts
- **Genetic Code and Codon Recognition** — The near-universal triplet code maps 61 sense codons to 20 amino acids via wobble-permissive codon-anticodon pairing.
- **Ribosome Structure and Function** — The two-subunit ribozyme-containing ribosome catalyzes peptide bond formation and translocates along the mRNA one codon at a time.
- **Translation Initiation** — Initiation factors assemble the small subunit, initiator tRNA, and mRNA at the start codon, the rate-limiting and most regulated step of translation.
- **Aminoacyl-tRNA Synthetases** — Enzymes that charge each tRNA with its cognate amino acid with high specificity, setting the fidelity ceiling of the genetic code.
- **Co-translational Quality Control** — Ribosome-associated chaperones and surveillance pathways such as no-go and non-stop decay clear stalled or aberrant translation complexes.

nodes.name: Gene Regulation and Signal-Responsive Expression
nodes.en: Investigates how cells switch genes on and off in response to developmental cues and environmental signals, spanning transcription factor logic, operons, enhancers, and combinatorial control. Explains how a single genome generates distinct cell types and adaptive responses. A cornerstone for developmental biology, immunology, and disease mechanisms.
nodes.brief:
Investigates how cells switch genes on and off in response to developmental cues and environmental signals, spanning transcription factor logic, operons, enhancers, and combinatorial control. Explains how a single genome generates distinct cell types and adaptive responses. A cornerstone for developmental biology, immunology, and disease mechanisms.

### Concepts
- **Transcription Factors and DNA Binding Motifs** — Sequence-specific proteins bind cis-regulatory elements to activate or repress nearby genes, often through structural motifs like helix-turn-helix or zinc fingers.
- **Operons and Bacterial Regulatory Logic** — Coordinately controlled gene clusters, exemplified by the lac operon, illustrate inducible and repressible regulatory circuits in prokaryotes.
- **Enhancers and Combinatorial Control** — Distal regulatory elements integrate multiple transcription factor inputs and loop to promoters to achieve precise, tissue-specific expression.
- **Signal Transduction to the Nucleus** — Extracellular signals relay through kinase cascades to activate latent transcription factors, coupling gene expression to cellular environment.
- **Gene Regulatory Networks** — Interlocking feedback and feedforward circuits among transcription factors produce robust, switch-like, or oscillatory expression dynamics.

nodes.name: RNA Biology and Non-Coding RNA Regulation
nodes.en: Covers the diverse catalytic and regulatory roles of RNA beyond its role as a coding intermediate, including microRNAs, long non-coding RNAs, riboswitches, and catalytic ribozymes. Explores RNA structure-function relationships and post-transcriptional control of gene expression. A rapidly expanding field since the discovery that most of the genome is transcribed into non-coding transcripts.
nodes.brief:
Covers the diverse catalytic and regulatory roles of RNA beyond its role as a coding intermediate, including microRNAs, long non-coding RNAs, riboswitches, and catalytic ribozymes. Explores RNA structure-function relationships and post-transcriptional control of gene expression. A rapidly expanding field since the discovery that most of the genome is transcribed into non-coding transcripts.

### Concepts
- **MicroRNA-Mediated Silencing** — Small ~22-nucleotide RNAs guide the RISC complex to complementary mRNAs, triggering translational repression or degradation.
- **Long Non-Coding RNAs** — Transcripts over 200 nucleotides that regulate chromatin state, transcription, or scaffolding without being translated, such as Xist in X-inactivation.
- **Riboswitches and Ribozymes** — RNA elements that directly sense metabolite concentration or catalyze chemical reactions, revealing pre-protein regulatory and catalytic capacities of RNA.
- **RNA Interference and Small Interfering RNAs** — A conserved sequence-specific gene-silencing pathway triggered by double-stranded RNA, widely repurposed as an experimental knockdown tool.
- **CRISPR RNA-Guided Immunity** — Bacterial adaptive immune systems use guide RNAs to direct Cas nucleases to cleave invading nucleic acids, the biological basis of CRISPR gene editing.

nodes.name: Chromatin Structure and Epigenetic Regulation
nodes.en: Studies how DNA is packaged with histones into chromatin and how covalent modifications to DNA and histones establish heritable gene expression states without altering sequence. Encompasses nucleosome positioning, histone codes, DNA methylation, and higher-order genome architecture. Explains cell-type memory, X-inactivation, and imprinting.
nodes.brief:
Studies how DNA is packaged with histones into chromatin and how covalent modifications to DNA and histones establish heritable gene expression states without altering sequence. Encompasses nucleosome positioning, histone codes, DNA methylation, and higher-order genome architecture. Explains cell-type memory, X-inactivation, and imprinting.

### Concepts
- **Nucleosome Structure and Positioning** — DNA wound around histone octamers forms the fundamental chromatin unit, whose positioning gates transcription factor access.
- **Histone Post-Translational Modifications** — Acetylation, methylation, and other marks on histone tails recruit reader proteins that activate or compact chromatin, forming a combinatorial 'histone code'.
- **DNA Methylation** — Covalent addition of methyl groups to cytosine, typically at CpG dinucleotides, silences gene expression and is heritable through cell division.
- **Chromatin Remodeling Complexes** — ATP-dependent machines slide, evict, or restructure nucleosomes to dynamically expose or occlude regulatory DNA.
- **Genomic Imprinting and X-Inactivation** — Parent-of-origin-specific silencing and dosage compensation mechanisms demonstrate epigenetic control operating at the whole-chromosome scale.

nodes.name: Molecular Techniques and Genome Engineering
nodes.en: Covers the experimental methods that made molecular biology a manipulable science: cloning, amplification, sequencing, and targeted genome editing. Includes the enzymatic and biophysical principles underlying PCR, recombinant DNA technology, next-generation sequencing, and CRISPR-based editing. These tools are the practical foundation for both basic research and biotechnology.
nodes.brief:
Covers the experimental methods that made molecular biology a manipulable science: cloning, amplification, sequencing, and targeted genome editing. Includes the enzymatic and biophysical principles underlying PCR, recombinant DNA technology, next-generation sequencing, and CRISPR-based editing. These tools are the practical foundation for both basic research and biotechnology.

### Concepts
- **Recombinant DNA and Molecular Cloning** — Restriction enzymes and ligases enable insertion of foreign DNA into plasmid vectors for propagation and expression in host cells.
- **Polymerase Chain Reaction** — A thermocycling enzymatic method exponentially amplifies a targeted DNA sequence from minute starting material.
- **DNA Sequencing Technologies** — Methods from Sanger dideoxy sequencing to massively parallel next-generation and long-read platforms determine nucleotide order at increasing scale and speed.
- **CRISPR-Cas Genome Editing** — Programmable Cas nucleases guided by engineered RNAs introduce targeted double-strand breaks for precise gene disruption, correction, or insertion.
- **Gene Expression Profiling** — Microarrays and RNA-sequencing quantify transcript abundance genome-wide, revealing expression differences across conditions or cell states.

nodes.name: Cell Biology
nodes.en: Cell biology is the study of the cell as the basic structural and functional unit of life, covering organelle architecture and compartmentalization, the cytoskeleton, membrane trafficking, the cell cycle, intracellular and intercellular signaling, and programmed cell death. It examines structure and behavior at the level of the whole cell, distinct from Molecular Biology's focus on nucleic-acid and protein mechanisms and Physiology's focus on tissue- and organ-level function.
nodes.cs: Buněčná biologie je studium buňky jako základní strukturní a funkční jednotky života, zahrnující architekturu organel a jejich kompartmentalizaci, cytoskelet, membránový transport, buněčný cyklus, nitrobuněčnou i mezibuněčnou signalizaci a programovanou buněčnou smrt. Zkoumá strukturu a chování na úrovni celé buňky, na rozdíl od molekulární biologie, zaměřené na mechanismy nukleových kyselin a proteinů, a fyziologie, jež se soustředí na funkci tkání a orgánů.

nodes.name: Membrane Structure & Dynamics
nodes.en: Studies the composition, organization, and biophysics of biological membranes — lipid bilayers, membrane proteins, and the compartmentalization they enforce. Covers lateral heterogeneity (lipid rafts), membrane curvature, fusion/fission mechanics, and transport across membranes. Foundational to understanding how cells maintain distinct internal environments and exchange material with their surroundings.
nodes.brief:
Studies the composition, organization, and biophysics of biological membranes — lipid bilayers, membrane proteins, and the compartmentalization they enforce. Covers lateral heterogeneity (lipid rafts), membrane curvature, fusion/fission mechanics, and transport across membranes. Foundational to understanding how cells maintain distinct internal environments and exchange material with their surroundings.

### Concepts
- **Lipid Bilayer** — The self-assembled phospholipid sheet that forms the basic permeability barrier of all cellular membranes.
- **Membrane Proteins** — Integral and peripheral proteins that mediate transport, signaling, and adhesion across the bilayer.
- **Membrane Fluidity** — The lateral and rotational mobility of lipids and proteins that governs membrane function and lipid raft formation.
- **Transport Across Membranes** — Passive diffusion, facilitated transport, and active pumping that move ions and solutes across the bilayer.

nodes.name: Organelle Biology & Compartmentalization
nodes.en: Examines the structure, biogenesis, and specialized functions of membrane-bound and membraneless organelles that subdivide the eukaryotic cell. Includes the endomembrane system, mitochondria and plastids, peroxisomes, and the nucleus, each maintaining distinct biochemical environments for specialized tasks. Organelle interaction via contact sites and inheritance during division are active research areas.
nodes.brief:
Examines the structure, biogenesis, and specialized functions of membrane-bound and membraneless organelles that subdivide the eukaryotic cell. Includes the endomembrane system, mitochondria and plastids, peroxisomes, and the nucleus, each maintaining distinct biochemical environments for specialized tasks. Organelle interaction via contact sites and inheritance during division are active research areas.

### Concepts
- **Nucleus** — The membrane-bound organelle housing chromatin and the machinery for transcription and RNA processing.
- **Mitochondria** — Semi-autonomous double-membrane organelles that generate ATP via oxidative phosphorylation and retain their own genome.
- **Endoplasmic Reticulum** — A continuous membrane network responsible for protein folding, lipid synthesis, and calcium storage.
- **Golgi Apparatus** — The stacked organelle that modifies, sorts, and packages proteins and lipids for their final destinations.
- **Peroxisomes** — Single-membrane organelles that carry out fatty acid oxidation and reactive oxygen species detoxification.

nodes.name: Cytoskeleton & Cell Motility
nodes.en: Covers the dynamic filament networks — actin, microtubules, and intermediate filaments — that give cells shape, mechanical resilience, and the capacity for movement. Includes motor proteins, cell polarity, and mechanotransduction, linking cytoskeletal architecture to migration, division, and tissue mechanics. Remains a frontier for understanding force generation and self-organization at the molecular scale.
nodes.brief:
Covers the dynamic filament networks — actin, microtubules, and intermediate filaments — that give cells shape, mechanical resilience, and the capacity for movement. Includes motor proteins, cell polarity, and mechanotransduction, linking cytoskeletal architecture to migration, division, and tissue mechanics. Remains a frontier for understanding force generation and self-organization at the molecular scale.

### Concepts
- **Actin Filaments** — Dynamic polymers that drive cell shape change, cortical tension, and lamellipodial protrusion.
- **Microtubules** — Polarized tubulin polymers that organize intracellular transport tracks and form the mitotic spindle.
- **Intermediate Filaments** — Rope-like polymers such as keratins and lamins that confer mechanical strength to cells and nuclei.
- **Motor Proteins** — ATP-driven proteins including myosins, kinesins, and dyneins that generate force along cytoskeletal tracks.
- **Cell Migration** — The coordinated cytoskeletal remodeling process that enables directed cell movement in development and disease.

nodes.name: Vesicular Trafficking & Membrane Sorting
nodes.en: Studies how proteins and lipids move between organelles and to the cell surface via coated vesicles, and how cargo is selectively packaged, targeted, and delivered. Encompasses the secretory pathway, endocytosis, autophagy, and the molecular machinery of vesicle budding and fusion (SNAREs, Rabs, coat proteins). Errors in trafficking underlie numerous inherited and neurodegenerative diseases.
nodes.brief:
Studies how proteins and lipids move between organelles and to the cell surface via coated vesicles, and how cargo is selectively packaged, targeted, and delivered. Encompasses the secretory pathway, endocytosis, autophagy, and the molecular machinery of vesicle budding and fusion (SNAREs, Rabs, coat proteins). Errors in trafficking underlie numerous inherited and neurodegenerative diseases.

### Concepts
- **Secretory Pathway** — The ER-to-Golgi-to-plasma-membrane route by which newly synthesized proteins are exported from the cell.
- **Endocytosis** — The process by which cells internalize surface material and extracellular cargo into vesicles.
- **SNARE Proteins** — Membrane proteins that catalyze the specific fusion of transport vesicles with target membranes.
- **Autophagy** — The self-degradative pathway that delivers cytoplasmic components to lysosomes for recycling.
- **Rab GTPases** — Molecular switches that confer identity and directionality to trafficking vesicles along their route.

nodes.name: Cell Cycle & Division
nodes.en: Addresses the ordered sequence of events by which a cell duplicates its contents and divides, including DNA replication licensing, checkpoint surveillance, and the mechanics of mitosis and cytokinesis. Cyclin-CDK oscillations and checkpoint proteins enforce fidelity, and their dysregulation is a hallmark of cancer. Meiosis and its distinct recombination and segregation machinery are treated as a specialized variant.
nodes.brief:
Addresses the ordered sequence of events by which a cell duplicates its contents and divides, including DNA replication licensing, checkpoint surveillance, and the mechanics of mitosis and cytokinesis. Cyclin-CDK oscillations and checkpoint proteins enforce fidelity, and their dysregulation is a hallmark of cancer. Meiosis and its distinct recombination and segregation machinery are treated as a specialized variant.

### Concepts
- **Cyclins & CDKs** — The oscillating kinase complexes that drive progression through the cell cycle's phases.
- **Checkpoints** — Surveillance mechanisms that halt cycle progression until DNA replication or chromosome attachment is complete.
- **Mitosis** — The process of nuclear division that segregates duplicated chromosomes equally into two daughter cells.
- **Cytokinesis** — The physical division of the cytoplasm that completes cell separation after nuclear division.
- **Meiosis** — The specialized two-division cycle that halves chromosome number and generates genetic recombination in gametes.

nodes.name: Cell Signaling & Communication
nodes.en: Examines how cells detect and respond to extracellular and intracellular cues via receptors, second messengers, and signal transduction cascades. Covers receptor classes, downstream kinase networks, and how signals are integrated to control gene expression, metabolism, and behavior. Central to understanding development, homeostasis, and disease-associated pathway rewiring.
nodes.brief:
Examines how cells detect and respond to extracellular and intracellular cues via receptors, second messengers, and signal transduction cascades. Covers receptor classes, downstream kinase networks, and how signals are integrated to control gene expression, metabolism, and behavior. Central to understanding development, homeostasis, and disease-associated pathway rewiring.

### Concepts
- **Receptor Tyrosine Kinases** — Cell-surface receptors that autophosphorylate upon ligand binding to launch downstream signaling cascades.
- **G Protein-Coupled Receptors** — The largest receptor family, transducing extracellular signals via heterotrimeric G proteins.
- **Second Messengers** — Small diffusible molecules such as cAMP and calcium that relay and amplify receptor signals intracellularly.
- **Signal Transduction Cascades** — Sequential kinase or phosphatase relays that convert a receptor event into a specific cellular response.

nodes.name: Cell Death & Stress Responses
nodes.en: Covers the regulated pathways by which cells self-destruct or adapt under stress, including apoptosis, necroptosis, pyroptosis, and the unfolded protein response. These programs balance tissue homeostasis against pathology, and their dysregulation contributes to cancer, neurodegeneration, and autoimmune disease. Distinguishing regulated cell death modalities from passive necrosis remains an active area of mechanistic refinement.
nodes.brief:
Covers the regulated pathways by which cells self-destruct or adapt under stress, including apoptosis, necroptosis, pyroptosis, and the unfolded protein response. These programs balance tissue homeostasis against pathology, and their dysregulation contributes to cancer, neurodegeneration, and autoimmune disease. Distinguishing regulated cell death modalities from passive necrosis remains an active area of mechanistic refinement.

### Concepts
- **Apoptosis** — The caspase-driven program of controlled cell dismantling that removes damaged or unneeded cells without inflammation.
- **Necroptosis** — A regulated, inflammatory form of cell death executed by RIPK/MLKL signaling as a backup to apoptosis.
- **Unfolded Protein Response** — The ER stress-sensing pathway that restores protein-folding homeostasis or triggers death if stress is unresolved.
- **Autophagic Cell Death** — A death modality in which excessive self-digestion via autophagy contributes to cell demise.

nodes.name: Genetics
nodes.en: Genetics is the study of heredity and hereditary variation: how discrete factors transmit from parents to offspring, recombine, and mutate, and how their frequencies shift across populations and generations. Spans Mendelian inheritance, chromosome mapping, mutation, population and quantitative genetics, epigenetic inheritance, and the molecular technologies that read and manipulate genomes. Distinct from Genomics & Bioinformatics, which analyzes genome-scale sequence data computationally rather than inheritance patterns themselves.
nodes.cs: Genetika je nauka o dědičnosti a dědičné proměnlivosti: o tom, jak se diskrétní vlohy přenášejí z rodičů na potomky, rekombinují a mutují, a jak se jejich četnost mění v populacích napříč generacemi. Zahrnuje mendelovskou dědičnost, mapování chromozomů, mutace, populační a kvantitativní genetiku, epigenetickou dědičnost a molekulární technologie pro čtení a úpravu genomů. Liší se od Genomiky a bioinformatiky, která genomová sekvenční data analyzuje výpočetně, nikoli dědičné vzorce samotné.

nodes.name: Mendelian and Transmission Genetics
nodes.en: Studies the rules by which discrete hereditary factors pass from parents to offspring, established by Mendel's crosses and extended to complex inheritance patterns. Covers segregation, independent assortment, dominance relationships, and pedigree analysis in humans and model organisms. Forms the conceptual foundation on which all later genetic theory is built.
nodes.brief:
Studies the rules by which discrete hereditary factors pass from parents to offspring, established by Mendel's crosses and extended to complex inheritance patterns. Covers segregation, independent assortment, dominance relationships, and pedigree analysis in humans and model organisms. Forms the conceptual foundation on which all later genetic theory is built.

### Concepts
- **Law of Segregation** — Each organism carries two alleles per gene that separate into different gametes during meiosis.
- **Independent Assortment** — Alleles of genes on different chromosomes segregate into gametes independently of one another.
- **Dominance and Codominance** — Allelic interactions range from complete dominance through incomplete dominance to codominance, shaping observed phenotype ratios.
- **Pedigree Analysis** — Family-tree diagrams trace trait transmission to infer mode of inheritance and estimate recurrence risk.
- **Non-Mendelian Inheritance** — Patterns such as X-linkage, mitochondrial inheritance, and genomic imprinting deviate from simple Mendelian ratios.

nodes.name: Chromosomal Behavior, Linkage, and Genetic Mapping
nodes.en: Examines how chromosomes physically carry and transmit genes, and how recombination frequency is used to order loci along a chromosome. Links cytological observation of meiosis to statistical inference of gene position. Underpins classical genetic maps and modern high-resolution recombination mapping alike.
nodes.brief:
Examines how chromosomes physically carry and transmit genes, and how recombination frequency is used to order loci along a chromosome. Links cytological observation of meiosis to statistical inference of gene position. Underpins classical genetic maps and modern high-resolution recombination mapping alike.

### Concepts
- **Linkage** — Genes located near one another on a chromosome tend to be inherited together rather than independently.
- **Crossing Over** — Homologous chromosomes exchange segments during meiotic prophase I, generating recombinant chromatids.
- **Recombination Frequency Mapping** — The percentage of recombinant offspring between two loci estimates their physical distance in map units.
- **Chromosome Theory of Heredity** — Genes are physical entities located at specific positions on chromosomes, first demonstrated in Drosophila.
- **Cytogenetic Mapping** — Microscopic and molecular karyotype analysis localizes genes and structural rearrangements to chromosome bands.

nodes.name: Mutation and Genome Variation
nodes.en: Covers the origin, types, and consequences of heritable changes in DNA sequence, from point mutations to large-scale structural and chromosomal rearrangements. Addresses mutation rate, repair evasion, and the balance between deleterious and adaptive variants in populations. Central to understanding disease etiology, genome evolution, and standing genetic diversity.
nodes.brief:
Covers the origin, types, and consequences of heritable changes in DNA sequence, from point mutations to large-scale structural and chromosomal rearrangements. Addresses mutation rate, repair evasion, and the balance between deleterious and adaptive variants in populations. Central to understanding disease etiology, genome evolution, and standing genetic diversity.

### Concepts
- **Point Mutation** — A single-nucleotide substitution, insertion, or deletion that can alter protein sequence or gene expression.
- **Structural Variation** — Duplications, deletions, inversions, and translocations rearrange large genomic segments and can disrupt gene function.
- **Aneuploidy and Polyploidy** — Abnormal chromosome number arising from meiotic nondisjunction or whole-genome duplication events.
- **Mutagenesis Mechanisms** — Replication errors, chemical mutagens, and radiation introduce sequence changes that repair pathways may fail to correct.
- **Transposable Elements** — Mobile DNA sequences that copy or relocate within the genome, generating insertional mutations and structural variants.

nodes.name: Population and Evolutionary Genetics
nodes.en: Analyzes allele and genotype frequency dynamics within and between populations under selection, drift, migration, and mutation. Provides the mathematical framework (Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium, coalescent theory) linking Mendelian inheritance to evolutionary change. Essential for conservation biology, molecular evolution, and reconstructing population history.
nodes.brief:
Analyzes allele and genotype frequency dynamics within and between populations under selection, drift, migration, and mutation. Provides the mathematical framework (Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium, coalescent theory) linking Mendelian inheritance to evolutionary change. Essential for conservation biology, molecular evolution, and reconstructing population history.

### Concepts
- **Hardy-Weinberg Equilibrium** — A null model predicting stable allele and genotype frequencies in an infinite, randomly mating population absent evolutionary forces.
- **Genetic Drift** — Random sampling of alleles across generations causes frequency fluctuations, especially pronounced in small populations.
- **Natural Selection Models** — Differential reproductive success of genotypes shifts allele frequencies according to selection coefficients and fitness.
- **Coalescent Theory** — A retrospective model tracing sampled alleles back to their most recent common ancestor to infer population history.
- **Molecular Evolution** — Compares DNA and protein sequence divergence across taxa to estimate mutation rates and phylogenetic relationships.

nodes.name: Quantitative and Complex Trait Genetics
nodes.en: Studies continuously varying traits governed by many loci of small effect interacting with environmental factors. Develops statistical tools—heritability estimation, QTL mapping, genome-wide association studies—to dissect the genetic architecture underlying complex phenotypes and disease risk. Bridges classical biometry with modern genomic data.
nodes.brief:
Studies continuously varying traits governed by many loci of small effect interacting with environmental factors. Develops statistical tools—heritability estimation, QTL mapping, genome-wide association studies—to dissect the genetic architecture underlying complex phenotypes and disease risk. Bridges classical biometry with modern genomic data.

### Concepts
- **Heritability** — The proportion of phenotypic variance in a population attributable to genetic rather than environmental variation.
- **QTL Mapping** — Statistical linkage of trait variation to chromosomal regions identifies quantitative trait loci influencing a phenotype.
- **Genome-Wide Association Studies** — Genome-wide marker scans correlate common variants with trait or disease status across large cohorts.
- **Polygenic Scores** — Aggregated weighted effects of many variants predict individual genetic liability for a complex trait.
- **Gene-Environment Interaction** — Phenotypic outcome of a genotype depends on environmental context, complicating simple genetic prediction.

nodes.name: Epigenetics and Regulatory Inheritance
nodes.en: Investigates heritable changes in gene expression that occur without altering the underlying DNA sequence, mediated by chromatin state and small RNAs. Examines how such marks are established, maintained through cell division, and in some cases transmitted across generations. A rapidly expanding field reshaping views of inheritance beyond strict DNA sequence transmission.
nodes.brief:
Investigates heritable changes in gene expression that occur without altering the underlying DNA sequence, mediated by chromatin state and small RNAs. Examines how such marks are established, maintained through cell division, and in some cases transmitted across generations. A rapidly expanding field reshaping views of inheritance beyond strict DNA sequence transmission.

### Concepts
- **DNA Methylation** — Addition of methyl groups to cytosine bases silences gene expression and is propagated through DNA replication.
- **Histone Modification** — Chemical marks on histone tails alter chromatin accessibility and regulate transcriptional activity.
- **Genomic Imprinting** — Parent-of-origin-specific epigenetic marks cause monoallelic expression of certain genes.
- **Transgenerational Epigenetic Inheritance** — Epigenetic states transmitted across multiple generations without corresponding DNA sequence change, still debated in mechanism and scope.
- **Non-Coding RNA Regulation** — microRNAs and long non-coding RNAs modulate gene expression post-transcriptionally and influence chromatin state.

nodes.name: Molecular Genetics and Genomic Technologies
nodes.en: Covers the experimental and computational methods used to sequence, edit, and manipulate genomes, from classical recombinant DNA techniques to CRISPR-based gene editing. Enables functional dissection of gene action and underlies modern genetic diagnostics, synthetic biology, and gene therapy. A fast-moving technical frontier that continually redefines what genetic questions are answerable.
nodes.brief:
Covers the experimental and computational methods used to sequence, edit, and manipulate genomes, from classical recombinant DNA techniques to CRISPR-based gene editing. Enables functional dissection of gene action and underlies modern genetic diagnostics, synthetic biology, and gene therapy. A fast-moving technical frontier that continually redefines what genetic questions are answerable.

### Concepts
- **DNA Sequencing** — Methods from Sanger chain-termination to massively parallel next-generation sequencing determine nucleotide order in a genome.
- **CRISPR-Cas Gene Editing** — RNA-guided nuclease systems enable targeted cutting and modification of specific genomic sequences.
- **Recombinant DNA Technology** — Cloning vectors and restriction enzymes allow isolation, amplification, and recombination of specific DNA fragments.
- **Functional Genomics** — High-throughput screens and expression profiling assign function to genes at genome-wide scale.
- **Gene Therapy** — Delivery of corrective genetic material into cells aims to treat disease caused by defective genes.

nodes.name: Genomics & Bioinformatics
nodes.en: Genomics and bioinformatics studies living systems at the scale of the whole genome through sequencing and computational analysis, spanning assembly and annotation, comparative and functional genomics, sequence algorithms, and multi-omics integration. Where Genetics traces the inheritance of individual traits, Genomics operates on whole-genome data, supplying the computational infrastructure and reference datasets the other biological disciplines draw on.
nodes.cs: Genomika a bioinformatika studuje živé systémy v měřítku celého genomu prostřednictvím sekvenování a výpočetní analýzy; zahrnuje sestavení a anotaci genomu, komparativní a funkční genomiku, sekvenční algoritmy a integraci multi-omických dat. Zatímco genetika sleduje dědičnost jednotlivých znaků, genomika pracuje s daty celého genomu a poskytuje výpočetní infrastrukturu a referenční datové sady, z nichž čerpají ostatní biologické obory.

nodes.name: Sequencing Technologies & Read Data
nodes.en: The experimental substrate of genomics: platforms, chemistries, and protocols that convert nucleic acids into digital read data. Short-read (Illumina), long-read (PacBio HiFi, Oxford Nanopore), and single-cell/spatial chemistries each impose distinct error profiles, coverage biases, and length distributions that downstream analysis must model. Platform choice determines what genomic questions — SNPs, structural variants, phasing, isoforms — are even answerable.
nodes.brief:
The experimental substrate of genomics: platforms, chemistries, and protocols that convert nucleic acids into digital read data. Short-read (Illumina), long-read (PacBio HiFi, Oxford Nanopore), and single-cell/spatial chemistries each impose distinct error profiles, coverage biases, and length distributions that downstream analysis must model. Platform choice determines what genomic questions — SNPs, structural variants, phasing, isoforms — are even answerable.

### Concepts
- **Short-Read Sequencing** — Illumina-style sequencing-by-synthesis produces high-accuracy reads of 50-300bp at low per-base cost.
- **Long-Read Sequencing** — PacBio HiFi and Oxford Nanopore generate kilobase-to-megabase reads that resolve repeats and structural variants.
- **Sequencing Error Models & Quality Scores** — Phred quality scores and platform-specific error profiles quantify per-base confidence for downstream calling.
- **Library Preparation & Target Enrichment** — Fragmentation, adapter ligation, and hybridization capture prepare and enrich DNA/RNA for specific sequencing goals.
- **Single-Cell Sequencing Chemistries** — Droplet- and plate-based barcoding protocols capture genome or transcriptome data from individual cells.
- **Spatial Sequencing** — In situ or barcoded-array methods preserve tissue coordinates while capturing transcript or genomic signal.

nodes.name: Genome Assembly & Annotation
nodes.en: Reconstructs contiguous genome sequences from fragmented read data and identifies the functional elements — genes, exons, regulatory regions — encoded within them. De novo and reference-guided assembly algorithms trade off computational cost against accuracy in resolving repeats, haplotypes, and structural variation. Annotation pipelines layer statistical and homology-based gene models onto finished assemblies to produce usable genome references.
nodes.brief:
Reconstructs contiguous genome sequences from fragmented read data and identifies the functional elements — genes, exons, regulatory regions — encoded within them. De novo and reference-guided assembly algorithms trade off computational cost against accuracy in resolving repeats, haplotypes, and structural variation. Annotation pipelines layer statistical and homology-based gene models onto finished assemblies to produce usable genome references.

### Concepts
- **De Novo Assembly** — Overlap-layout-consensus and de Bruijn graph algorithms reconstruct genomes without a reference sequence.
- **Reference-Guided Assembly & Mapping** — Reads are aligned against an existing reference genome to reconstruct or scaffold a new sample's sequence.
- **Gene Prediction & Annotation Pipelines** — Ab initio and evidence-based models identify gene structures, exon boundaries, and functional elements in assembled DNA.
- **Genome Quality Assessment** — Metrics like N50 and BUSCO completeness scores benchmark assembly contiguity and gene-content coverage.
- **Pangenome Graphs** — Graph-based references represent shared and variable sequence across many individuals of a species.
- **Structural Variant Detection** — Algorithms identify large insertions, deletions, inversions, and translocations from assembled or aligned data.

nodes.name: Comparative & Evolutionary Genomics
nodes.en: Compares genome sequences across species and lineages to infer evolutionary history, conserved function, and adaptive change. Synteny mapping and orthology inference trace how genomic content is rearranged and retained over evolutionary time, while phylogenomic methods reconstruct species relationships from whole-genome data. This sub-area underpins functional annotation transfer and the identification of lineage-specific innovation.
nodes.brief:
Compares genome sequences across species and lineages to infer evolutionary history, conserved function, and adaptive change. Synteny mapping and orthology inference trace how genomic content is rearranged and retained over evolutionary time, while phylogenomic methods reconstruct species relationships from whole-genome data. This sub-area underpins functional annotation transfer and the identification of lineage-specific innovation.

### Concepts
- **Synteny & Orthology Inference** — Conserved gene order and sequence similarity identify orthologous and paralogous genes across genomes.
- **Phylogenomics** — Whole-genome or multi-locus sequence data reconstructs evolutionary trees among species or populations.
- **Molecular Evolution & Selection Signatures** — dN/dS ratios and related statistics detect purifying, neutral, or positive selection acting on coding sequence.
- **Ancestral Genome Reconstruction** — Comparative algorithms infer the gene content and arrangement of extinct ancestral genomes.
- **Comparative Annotation of Regulatory Elements** — Cross-species conservation of non-coding sequence identifies functionally constrained regulatory regions.

nodes.name: Functional Genomics & Gene Regulation
nodes.en: Studies gene function and regulation at genome scale, linking DNA sequence to expression output and phenotype. RNA-seq, ChIP-seq, and CRISPR-based screens generate genome-wide readouts of transcription, protein-DNA binding, and gene essentiality. Network inference methods integrate these readouts into models of regulatory circuitry governing cell state and identity.
nodes.brief:
Studies gene function and regulation at genome scale, linking DNA sequence to expression output and phenotype. RNA-seq, ChIP-seq, and CRISPR-based screens generate genome-wide readouts of transcription, protein-DNA binding, and gene essentiality. Network inference methods integrate these readouts into models of regulatory circuitry governing cell state and identity.

### Concepts
- **RNA-Seq & Transcriptomics** — High-throughput sequencing of RNA quantifies genome-wide transcript abundance and isoform structure.
- **ChIP-Seq & Regulatory Element Mapping** — Chromatin immunoprecipitation followed by sequencing localizes transcription-factor binding and histone marks genome-wide.
- **CRISPR Functional Screens** — Pooled CRISPR knockout or activation libraries systematically test gene function across the genome.
- **Gene Regulatory Network Inference** — Computational models reconstruct regulatory relationships between transcription factors and target genes from expression data.
- **Alternative Splicing Analysis** — Transcriptomic methods identify and quantify exon-inclusion variants generated from a single gene locus.

nodes.name: Sequence Algorithms & Computational Methods
nodes.en: The algorithmic and statistical core underlying all genomic data analysis, independent of any single biological question. Alignment, indexing, and probabilistic models provide the computational primitives that assembly, variant calling, and annotation pipelines build upon. Machine learning methods increasingly augment or replace classical statistical models across this layer.
nodes.cs: Algoritmické a statistické jádro veškeré analýzy genomických dat, nezávislé na konkrétní biologické otázce. Zarovnávání sekvencí, indexování a pravděpodobnostní modely poskytují výpočetní primitivy, na nichž stavějí sestavování genomu, volání variant a anotační pipeline. Metody strojového učení stále více doplňují nebo nahrazují klasické statistické modely v této vrstvě.
nodes.brief:
The algorithmic and statistical core underlying all genomic data analysis, independent of any single biological question. Alignment, indexing, and probabilistic models provide the computational primitives that assembly, variant calling, and annotation pipelines build upon. Machine learning methods increasingly augment or replace classical statistical models across this layer.

### Concepts
- **Sequence Alignment Algorithms** — Dynamic-programming and heuristic methods like Smith-Waterman and BWA-MEM align reads to references or to each other.
- **Suffix Arrays & FM-Index** — Compressed full-text indexing structures enable rapid exact and approximate substring search across genomes.
- **Hidden Markov Models for Genomics** — Probabilistic state models underlie gene prediction, profile alignment, and sequence-motif detection.
- **Machine Learning for Sequence Analysis** — Deep learning architectures, including transformer-based genomic language models, predict function directly from sequence.
- **Variant Calling Statistics** — Bayesian and likelihood-based models distinguish true genetic variants from sequencing and alignment error.

nodes.name: Multi-Omics Integration & Systems Genomics
nodes.en: Combines genomic data with transcriptomic, proteomic, epigenomic, and metabolomic layers to build systems-level models of cellular and organismal biology. Integration methods must reconcile heterogeneous data types, scales, and noise structures to reveal cross-layer relationships invisible to any single omics modality. This frontier is central to precision medicine and mechanistic disease modeling.
nodes.cs: Kombinuje genomická data s transkriptomickou, proteomickou, epigenomickou a metabolomickou vrstvou a buduje tak systémové modely buněčné a organismální biologie. Integrační metody musí sladit heterogenní typy dat, měřítka a šumové struktury, aby odhalily mezivrstvé vztahy neviditelné pro žádnou jednotlivou omickou modalitu. Tato hraniční oblast je klíčová pro personalizovanou medicínu a mechanistické modelování nemocí.
nodes.brief:
Combines genomic data with transcriptomic, proteomic, epigenomic, and metabolomic layers to build systems-level models of cellular and organismal biology. Integration methods must reconcile heterogeneous data types, scales, and noise structures to reveal cross-layer relationships invisible to any single omics modality. This frontier is central to precision medicine and mechanistic disease modeling.

### Concepts
- **Multi-Omics Data Integration Methods** — Statistical and machine-learning frameworks jointly model genomic, transcriptomic, and proteomic datasets.
- **Single-Cell Multi-Omics** — Simultaneous profiling of genome, transcriptome, and epigenome within individual cells reveals coupled regulatory states.
- **Network-Based Systems Biology** — Graph models of molecular interactions integrate multi-omics evidence into predictive pathway and disease models.
- **Genotype-Phenotype Multi-Layer Models** — Computational frameworks link germline variation through intermediate omics layers to organismal phenotype.
- **Multi-Omics Data Harmonization Standards** — Shared ontologies and file formats enable reproducible cross-study integration of heterogeneous omics datasets.

nodes.name: Population & Medical Genomics
nodes.en: Applies genomic analysis to variation across human and non-human populations and its translation into clinical and biomedical insight. Population genetics quantifies allele frequency dynamics and demographic history, while medical genomics links variants to disease risk through association studies and clinical interpretation frameworks. This sub-area is the primary bridge between genomic research and healthcare application.
nodes.cs: Aplikuje genomickou analýzu na variabilitu v lidských i nelidských populacích a její převod do klinických a biomedicínských poznatků. Populační genetika kvantifikuje dynamiku frekvencí alel a demografickou historii, zatímco medicínská genomika propojuje varianty s rizikem onemocnění pomocí asociačních studií a klinických interpretačních rámců. Tato podoblast je hlavním mostem mezi genomickým výzkumem a jeho uplatněním ve zdravotnictví.
nodes.brief:
Applies genomic analysis to variation across human and non-human populations and its translation into clinical and biomedical insight. Population genetics quantifies allele frequency dynamics and demographic history, while medical genomics links variants to disease risk through association studies and clinical interpretation frameworks. This sub-area is the primary bridge between genomic research and healthcare application.

### Concepts
- **Population Genetics & Allele Frequency Analysis** — Statistical methods characterize genetic diversity, drift, migration, and demographic history within and among populations.
- **Genome-Wide Association Studies** — GWAS test genome-wide markers for statistical association with traits or disease across large cohorts.
- **Clinical Variant Interpretation** — Standardized frameworks classify the pathogenicity of individual genetic variants for diagnostic use.
- **Polygenic Risk Scores** — Aggregate weighted models of many small-effect variants estimate an individual's genetic disease liability.
- **Cancer Genomics** — Somatic mutation and copy-number analysis characterizes tumor evolution, driver genes, and treatment targets.

nodes.name: Epigenomics & Chromatin Genomics
nodes.en: Studies genome-wide chromatin state, DNA methylation, and three-dimensional nuclear architecture as regulators of gene expression independent of sequence change. Genome-wide assays map accessible chromatin, histone modifications, and long-range chromosomal contacts that shape cell identity. These layers explain how identical genomes produce divergent cell states and are heritable across cell divisions.
nodes.cs: Studuje celogenomový stav chromatinu, metylaci DNA a trojrozměrnou architekturu jádra jako regulátory genové exprese nezávislé na změně sekvence. Celogenomové metody mapují přístupný chromatin, histonové modifikace a dálkové chromozomové kontakty, které formují buněčnou identitu. Tyto vrstvy vysvětlují, jak identické genomy dávají vzniknout odlišným buněčným stavům a jsou dědičné napříč buněčnými děleními.
nodes.brief:
Studies genome-wide chromatin state, DNA methylation, and three-dimensional nuclear architecture as regulators of gene expression independent of sequence change. Genome-wide assays map accessible chromatin, histone modifications, and long-range chromosomal contacts that shape cell identity. These layers explain how identical genomes produce divergent cell states and are heritable across cell divisions.

### Concepts
- **DNA Methylation Profiling** — Bisulfite and long-read-based methods map cytosine methylation genome-wide as a heritable regulatory mark.
- **Chromatin Accessibility Assays** — ATAC-seq and related methods identify open chromatin regions accessible to regulatory proteins.
- **3D Genome Architecture** — Hi-C and related chromosome-conformation methods map long-range DNA contacts and topological domains.
- **Histone Modification Mapping** — Genome-wide profiling of histone marks characterizes chromatin states associated with activation or repression.
- **Epigenetic Inheritance** — Studies the transmission of chromatin and methylation states across cell divisions and, in some cases, generations.

nodes.name: Developmental Biology
nodes.en: Developmental biology traces how a single fertilized cell generates a complete, patterned organism, encompassing embryogenesis, morphogenesis, and the molecular signaling that specifies cell fate and body plan. It integrates genetics and cell biology into a temporal, whole-organism framework covering stem cell potency, tissue regeneration, and the evolutionary conservation of developmental programs (evo-devo).
nodes.cs: Vývojová biologie zkoumá, jak z jediné oplozené buňky vzniká kompletní, prostorově uspořádaný organismus, a zahrnuje embryogenezi, morfogenezi a molekulární signalizaci určující osud buněk a tělní plán. Integruje genetiku a buněčnou biologii do časového, celoorganismálního rámce zahrnujícího potenci kmenových buněk, regeneraci tkání a evoluční konzervaci vývojových programů (evo-devo).

nodes.name: Fertilization and Early Cleavage
nodes.en: Covers the events converting gametes into a totipotent zygote and the initial cell divisions that partition cytoplasm into a multicellular embryo. Establishes species-specific cleavage geometry, the maternal-to-zygotic transition, and the earliest axis-determining cytoplasmic asymmetries. Foundational to all downstream patterning because cleavage-stage errors propagate through every later stage.
nodes.cs: Zahrnuje děje přeměňující gamety v totipotentní zygotu a počáteční buněčná dělení, která rozdělují cytoplazmu do mnohobuněčného embrya. Ustavuje druhově specifickou geometrii rýhování, přechod z maternální na zygotickou kontrolu exprese genů a nejranější cytoplazmatické asymetrie určující tělní osy. Je základem veškerého následného prostorového uspořádání (patterningu), protože chyby ve fázi rýhování se šíří do všech pozdějších stadií.
nodes.brief:
Covers the events converting gametes into a totipotent zygote and the initial cell divisions that partition cytoplasm into a multicellular embryo. Establishes species-specific cleavage geometry, the maternal-to-zygotic transition, and the earliest axis-determining cytoplasmic asymmetries. Foundational to all downstream patterning because cleavage-stage errors propagate through every later stage.

### Concepts
- **Fertilization** — The fusion of sperm and egg triggers calcium waves, cortical granule release, and activation of the egg's developmental program.
- **Cleavage Patterns** — Radial, spiral, and rotational cleavage schemes distribute maternal cytoplasmic determinants into distinct blastomere lineages across taxa.
- **Maternal-to-Zygotic Transition** — Control of development switches from maternally deposited RNAs and proteins to transcription from the embryo's own genome.
- **Cytoplasmic Determinants** — Localized maternal mRNAs and proteins asymmetrically segregated at cleavage specify blastomere fate before zygotic transcription begins.
- **Blastula Formation** — Repeated cleavage divisions produce a hollow or compact blastula, the substrate on which gastrulation movements act.

nodes.name: Axis Specification and Body Patterning
nodes.en: Studies how embryos establish anterior-posterior, dorsal-ventral, and left-right coordinate systems and translate morphogen gradients into spatially ordered gene expression. Homeotic and Hox gene collinearity, positional information theory, and reaction-diffusion models explain how uniform fields of cells acquire distinct regional identities. This is the conceptual core linking molecular genetics to macroscopic body plan.
nodes.cs: Studuje, jak embrya ustavují předozadní, hřbetnobřišní a levopravou souřadnou soustavu a jak převádějí gradienty morfogenů na prostorově uspořádanou genovou expresi. Kolinearita homeotických a Hox genů, teorie poziční informace a reakčně-difuzní modely vysvětlují, jak jednotná pole buněk získávají odlišnou regionální identitu. Jde o pojmové jádro propojující molekulární genetiku s makroskopickým tělním plánem.
nodes.brief:
Studies how embryos establish anterior-posterior, dorsal-ventral, and left-right coordinate systems and translate morphogen gradients into spatially ordered gene expression. Homeotic and Hox gene collinearity, positional information theory, and reaction-diffusion models explain how uniform fields of cells acquire distinct regional identities.
